description_long:
Modelgrid sells inference, not instances: machine-learning models deployed as autoscaling API endpoints, billed for the seconds they actually compute. Anything with a PyTorch or vLLM serving path qualifies — language models, embedding models, diffusion models, speech-to-text — brought either as a container or a Python function, with an HTTPS endpoint on the other side.

The technical wedge is the cold start. Serverless GPU platforms historically forced a choice between keeping expensive GPUs warm around the clock and accepting cold starts of a minute or more while weights load. Modelgrid's answer is a snapshot architecture: it checkpoints the fully-initialized model — weights, CUDA context, allocator state — into a distributed cache that sits next to the GPUs, and restoring a snapshot brings a model from zero to serving in under 2 seconds, even for models tens of gigabytes in size. That claim, if it holds across model classes, dissolves the main argument for always-on capacity.

Beyond the snapshot trick, the platform covers the operational middle: batching, queueing, autoscaling from zero, and GPU selection across A100 and H100 classes. The intended customer is a team shipping AI features with spiky or unpredictable traffic — overnight batch jobs, consumer apps with regional peaks, agents that fan out to many models — where per-second billing means a model serving 10 minutes of traffic a day costs minutes, not hours.

The company positions itself as an owner-operator rather than a reseller: founded in San Francisco in 2023 by infrastructure engineers from a large GPU cloud, it says it runs its own capacity in three US regions and publishes status and latency numbers openly. The site does not name customers or disclose funding, so scale has to be read from the infrastructure claims rather than a logo wall. As a profile, it is a focused bet that the economics of GPU serving are still broken enough — idle capacity on one side, minute-long cold starts on the other — for a snapshot-restore architecture to be a company rather than a feature.
